coredata.dc:title: Performance enhancement of dye-sensitized solar cells based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Anjan
Ameer, Sabah Auda Abdul
Mohealdeen, Sura Mohammad
Hasoon, Ahmed
Abdulsayed, Yousra Ali
Lagum, Abdelmajeed Adam
Al-Ma'abreh, Alaa M.
Kadhim, Mustafa M.
coredata.dc:description: Photosensitizers can impact on the efficiency of dye-sensitized solar cells (DYSCs) by adjusting their electromechanical and optical attributes, and because of this they are considered important. Thus, they should satisfy certain requirements in order for DYSCs to operate efficiently. In the present research, a photosensitizer, Flavan-3-ol was proposed and graphene quantum dots doped with the nitrogen atom (N-GQDs) were used to modify its attributes. The electronic, optical and geometric attributes of this photosensitizer was probed using DFT as well as time-dependent DFT. In order to validate the basis set and the density functional used in the present study, we used the available data on the parent Flavan-3-ol. There was a substantial narrowing in the energy gap of Flavan-3-ol after hybridization. As a result, there was a shift in the absorption of Flavan-3-ol, i.e., it changed from the UV region to the visible region, thus matching the solar spectrum. Moreover, a light-harvesting efficiency near unity was achieved after an increase in the intensity of absorption intensity, which is capable of increasing the generation of current. We adjusted the newly-designed dye nanocomposites’ energy levels were closely aligned with reduction potential and the conduction band, which indicated the possibility of injecting and regenerating electrons. Based on the attributes of the materials, it can be confirmed that they meet the requirements and can be used in DYSCs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dye-sensitized solar cells
Graphene quantum dots
Flavan-3-ol
Absorption intensity
Reduction potential

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The structures of NCs and BEs
UV–visible absorption as well as emission transition analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimized geometries of (a) isolated F3Os, (b) GQD, (c) G-F3Os, (d) N1-G-F3Os, and (e) N2-G-F3Os.
The of molecular electrostatic maps (MEPs) of (a) N1-G-F3Os, and (b) N2-G-F3Os.
Energy levels alignment diagram for isolated F3Os, all the NCs, TiO2 CB, and I- /I-3 reduction potential.
DOS graph of N1-G-F3Os (a) and N2-G-F3Os (b).
The vertical excited energies and their oscillator strengths calculated at M06-2X /6–31 + G (d,p) with CPCM, methanol solvent.
Emission transitions wavelengths λmax in nm, oscillator strength f, Stokes shift λss in nm, and short-circuit current density JSC in mA.cm-2.

head.ce:title: Performance enhancement of dye-sensitized solar cells based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photosensitizers can impact on the efficiency of dye-sensitized solar cells (DYSCs) by adjusting their electromechanical and optical attributes, and because of this they are considered important. Thus, they should satisfy certain requirements in order for DYSCs to operate efficiently. In the present research, a photosensitizer, Flavan-3-ol was proposed and graphene quantum dots doped with the nitrogen atom (N-GQDs) were used to modify its attributes. The electronic, optical and geometric attributes of this photosensitizer was probed using DFT as well as time-dependent DFT. In order to validate the basis set and the density functional used in the present study, we used the available data on the parent Flavan-3-ol. There was a substantial narrowing in the energy gap of Flavan-3-ol after hybridization. As a result, there was a shift in the absorption of Flavan-3-ol, i.e., it changed from the UV region to the visible region, thus matching the solar spectrum. Moreover, a light-harvesting efficiency near unity was achieved after an increase in the intensity of absorption intensity, which is capable of increasing the generation of current. We adjusted the newly-designed dye nanocomposites’ energy levels were closely aligned with reduction potential and the conduction band, which indicated the possibility of injecting and regenerating electrons. Based on the attributes of the materials, it can be confirmed that they meet the requirements and can be used in DYSCs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The discovery of new renewable energy sources is of utmost importance due to the overconsumption of existing energy sources, which poses a threat to both human life and the environment. Solar energy is a highly promising alternative to fossil fuels and represents a clean source of energy. Photovoltaic solar cells (SCs) are used to harness solar energy by absorbing photons from solar radiation and converting them into electrical energy [1–9]. Based on the technology they use, their-generation SCs fall into 4 types, namely concentrated SCs, dye-sensitized SCs and polymer-based SCs [10–16]. Researchers and scientists have introduced such technologies for the sake of improving the efficiency of SCs. Nonetheless, the major obstacles to commercializing such SCs are their stability and short lifetime under long-time operations [17–19]. As mentioned above, one of the encouraging types of SCs is dye-sensitized solar cells (hereafter DYSCs) since there are eco-friendly, cheap and easy to synthesize [20–25]. To produce DYSCs, a semiconducting photoanode coated with a layer of dye/photosensitizer is typically employed. This layer includes a redox electrolyte, as well as a counter electrode and working electrode [1,2,26–30].

In recent years, many research groups have turned their focus on natural dyes on a TiO2 photoanode [4,31–34]. Through 3 anthocyanin dyes extracted from pomegranate (POM) fruit, Melastoma malabathricum (MM) fruit and Begonia malabarica Lam. (BM) leaves, a DYSC was fabricated in in ethanol without purifying by Singh et al. [3]. There was an increase in the open-circuit voltage of the dyes as follows: BM (500 mV) > POM (480 mV) > MM (380 mV). Also, an increase was observed in the short-circuit current density in a similar order. Also, open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current density increased the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of the dyes in a similar order: BM (1.76%) > POM (1.12%) > MM (0.76%) [3]. In a piece of research, dyes extracted from Phytolacca americana stalk (SPA), Phytolacca americana fruit (FPA), Berberis vulgaris root (RBV) and Berberis vulgaris fruit (FBV) were used without modifying by Güzel et al. [31]. For both SPA and FPA, betalain, isoquinoline alkaloid in RBV, and anthocyanin in FBV were the main dyes in each extract. The extracts demonstrated electrochemical and optical attributes suitable for use as photosensitizers in DYSCs. Both SPA and FPA exhibited broad absorption peaks, which enhances their light-harvesting capacity (LHC). A high LHC can improve the performance of DYSCs by generating greater short-circuit current density. Hence, the short-circuit current density of SPA and FPA (8.57–9.08 mA cm−2) was higher than that of RBV and FBV (5.47–7.46 mA cm−2). Also, the open-circuit voltage of SPA and FPA was higher than that of RBV and FBV. The enhancement in both open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current density resulted in an efficiency of 3.04% from the SPA extract, which was the highest [31].

The PCE of natural dyes is low in spite of their unique advantages and attributes [10]. Recently, researchers have found that graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can be used in order to improve the LHE of photosensitizers [35–38]. For example, several natural dye NCs hybridized with GQDs were developed by Gao et al. [35–37]. Another study was carried out into the hybridization of 6 carbazole-carbazole dyes containing various pi-linkers using GQDs at the theory levels of TD-CAM-B3LYP/ 6–311 + G(d,p) as well as B3LYP/6-31G (d,p) in tetrahydrofuran through PCM [36]. The driving force of GQD-hybridized nanocomposites (hereafter NCs) (-2.27 eV to −2.32 eV) was larger compared to that of isolated dyes (-1.43 eV to −2.06 eV). There was also an increase in the intensity of absorption, which resulted in a higher LHE (0.995%–0.998%) compared to the LHE of isolated dyes (0.916%–0.971%). As a result, NCs with a higher LHE generated more short-circuit current density compared to isolated dyes [37]. One of the suitable photosensitizers fabricated for application in DYSCs is the tetraphenyl-porphyrin (TPP) dye whose hybridization was carried out by using GQDs and graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) which had varying numbers of hydroxyl groups [37]. A theoretical study was carried out on the TPP dyes in the tetrahydrofuran solvent. The charge separation and optical attributes of the NCs were great. GOQD-(18OH)-TPP was the most suitable photosensitizer amongst other NCs since its LHE (0.998%), VOC (1.47 eV) and driving force (- 2.44 eV) were higher [37]. Using GQDs, a DYSC was made via phenoxazine-based dyes containing pyrrole (POXP) π-spacers and benzene (POXB) [35]. Moreover, Gao et al. used 8 anthoxanthin dyes, namely Quercetin, Kaempferol, Galangin, 3-Hydroxyflavone, Luteolin, Apigenin and Chrysin, to hybridize GQDs in the tetrahydrofuran solvent using the PCM [2]. The isolated dyes’ absorption maxima (187.19–363.81 nm) changed to a longer wavelength (449.05–456.42 nm) with a higher intensity to 1.7432 because of the NCs. Therefore, there was an improvement in the LHE from 0.6396% to 0.9819%. GQD- Chrysin and GQD-Flavone were only suitably aligned with the TiO2 CB after they were coupled with TiO2, which made them the most suitable nano-composites amongst others [2]. One of the members of the flavonoid family and polyphenol compounds is flavan-3-ols (F3Os) [39,40]. It is possible to extract F3Os from algae, herbs, vegetables and fruit. F3Os as well as their polymeric condensation products are considered useful ingredients in supplements, herbal medications, processed and whole foods, as well as different beverages be. The physical and chemical attributes of all flavonoids are the same. It is possible to use them in organic solvents like ethanol, methanol and water because of their organic molecule. The maximum absorbance of flavonoids range from 260 nm to 285 nm [40].

So, the current research aims at hybridizing F3Os using GQDs for fabricating NCs. First, GQDs were functionalized using nitrogen atoms by doping the NCs. We also compared the attributed of 12 NCs with each other and with those ofF3Os. The attributes, which consisted of intramolecular charge transfer (ICT), reorganization energies, electrochemical parameters, emission attributes, density of states (DOS), frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs) and binding energies, were computed and were discussed thoroughly. The energy gap (hereafter EG), which was capable of shifting the isolated catechin absorption from the U-V region to a longer wavelength in the visible region (VR), was tuned by hybridizing F3Os using GQDs, which in turn made it possible for all F3Os NCs to be used as a promising photosensitizer in DYSCs.

The molecular optimization computations in the present study were performed by employing DFT using the GAMESS software [41] and the M06-2X/6-311G(d,p) [42,43]. We performed a conformational search for identifying the structure with most stability because the molecules exhibited several conformations. We also performed frequency computations to verify the fact the optimized structures corresponded to the minimum on the potential energy surface (PES). The maximum absorption value of the reference molecules was adjusted with maximum absorption value that was computed experimentally. A nearer λ max to the experimental values is obtained when using M06-2X, so it was applied alongside with 6–311 + G(d,p) to perform the TD-DFT computations.In order to take into consideration the impact of solvation upon the absorption spectra in methanol, we employed the SCRF-PCM [44]. The absorption maximum wavelength of F3Os has been computed to be 280 nm [45,46], which is higher than the values computed in this study. The software used to delineate the DOS plots was GaussSum [47]. For the sake of comprehending the influence of hybridization on the energy levels and EGs, we can use the DOS plots. Tuning the EGs can change several photoelectronic parameters like absorption maxima. We can estimate the stability of NCs by computing their binding energy (Eb) (hereafter BE). The higher the BE, the more the stability. The binding of the NCs can be computed as follows: [48]:
(1)
E b
=
( n
E C
+ n
E O
+
E N
)
-
E
system
total
n u m b e r
o f
a t o m s
Where nEN, nEO, nEH and nEC respectively, represent the atomic energy of nitrogen, oxygen, hydrogen and carbon.

The sunlight-absorbing capability is commonly referred to as the LHE, which is capable of improving the short-circuit current density (JSC), which was computed as follows [48,49].
(2)
LHE = 1 -
10
- f
Here, f represents the maximum strength of an oscillator associated with the maximum wavelengths of absorption peaks.

We can compute JSC as follows [50]:.
(3)
J
sc
= e ∫
LHE
( λ )
ϕ
inj
η
coll
ϕ
ph . A M I 1.5 G
( λ )
d
( λ )
where φinj signifies the efficiency of electron injection, ηcoll signifies the efficiency of charge collection, and φph.AM1.5G signifies the solar radiation intensity standard reference in mAcm-2nm−1.

The maximum voltage without flowing current throughout the solar cells can be defined as the open-circuit voltage (VOC). In theory, VOC describes the electrons injection from the LUMO of photosensitizers as a donor to the conduction bands (C-B) of semiconducting TiO2 electrode as an acceptor. VOC can be computed as follows: [50].
(4)
V
OC
=
E
LUMO
-
E
CB
e
where e signifies the electron charge (1 eV), ELUMO signifies the LUMO energy of the photosensitizer and ECB signifies the energy of the C-B of TiO2.

There is a direct relationship between the power conversion efficiency (η) and JSC, VOC and fill factor (FF), which show the maximum output power (Pmax). We can compute the PCE as follows [51]:
(5)
η =
FF
V
oc
J
Sc
P
inc
=
P max
P
inc
Where Pinc signifies the intensity of solar radiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The structures of NCs and BEs
ce:section.ce:para:
Figs. 1 and 2
depict the Molecular Electrostatic Potential (MEP) maps of all NCs, as well as the optimized geometries of isolated F3Os and GQD-hybridized NCs. The first modeled nanocomposite was GQD-hybridized-3FOs (G-F3Os), which is shown in Fig. 2. This composite induced some polarity in the NC lattice, which improved electron transport characteristics. Different nitrogen configurations were used to dope the G-F3Os NCs, including doping the terminal N at the edge (N2-G-F3Os) and doping the central substitutional N (N1-G-F3Os). After doping with N, the lattice of GQD acquired p-type attributes, which increased the overall mobility of the NCs.

The computed BE values were all positive, which indicated that all of them are stable. The BE valued ranged from 6.34 to 6.94 eV, which showed the unsubstantial impact of the doping N. The BE of the nanocomposite with in-plane doped systems was higher because there was no distortion in the sp2 hybridization of the lattice of GQD. The stability of the N in-plane doping at the edge was more compared to the center because the induced strain was less, which was in line with results achieved by Agnoli, S and Favaro, M [52]. As a result, the BE of N2-G-F3Os at the edge (6.94 eV eV) was higher than the BE of N1-G-F3Os at the centre (6.34 eV).

Fig. 3 shows the FMOs of all photosensitizers along with their energy level alignment with the I- /I-3 reduction potential as well as the C-B of TiO2. Regeneration of dyes and injection of electrons are 2 processes in electron transport. As shown in Fig. 3, these two processes are both spontaneous. There was an injection of excited free electrons from the LUMOs of the photosensitizers to the conduction band. At the same time, there was a regeneration of electrons from the reduction potential to the HOMOs of the photosensitizers for the sake of reducing the oxidized dye. So, the LUMOs of the NCs have to be less negative than the C-B and their HOMOs have to be more negative than the reduction potential for the sake of allowing allow electrons to transport. According to Fig. 3, the LUMOs ranged from −3.35 eV to −2.42 eV, which were less negative than the conduction band (4.3 eV) [49], which confirmed the process of the injection of electrons. The HOMOs ranged from −5.29 to −4.95 eV, which were more negative than the reduction potential (-4.8 eV) [48], which confirmed that the systems had the capability of electron regeneration. According to Fig. 4 , there was a stabilization in the LUMO of F3Os after it was hybridized with GQDs. Doping N further stabilized G-F3Os. Moreover, the LUMOs of all NCs became more stable after doping N. When there is decrease in the energy difference between the LUMOs and the conduction band, the injection of electrons into the C-B becomes easier because a smaller amount of energy is required for transferring electrons [53]. The energy levels can be stabilized in order to avoid the loss of energy, mismatching the LUMOs and HOMOs, respectively, of the photosensitizer with the conduction band and the electrolyte [54]. In a similar vein, when there is decrease in the separation between the conduction and the HOMOs, and regeneration of electrons becomes easier. The distance between the LUMO of the F3Os and C-B was 3.73 eV but it did not exceed 1.80 eV between the LUMOs and the C-B (EL-CB). When the energy levels were stabilized, electron recombination was prevented and electron injection was improved. The energy difference between the reduction potential and the HOMOs of the photosensitizers of F3Os RP was 1.43 eV, where was the difference between the HOMOs and the reduction potential was 0.51 eV, which was lower than the reduction potential as a maximum difference, which demonstrated that NCs were more ideal photosensitizers compared to isolated F3Os for electron regeneration from the reduction potential. As shown in Fig. 4, with only a difference of 0.95 eV, the LUMO of N1-G-F3Os was aligned with the conduction band more appropriately contrary to N2-G-F3Os, which was away from the conduction band by 1.88 eV CB. A narrower EG requires less energy for exciting electrons to the excited state from the ground state. So, a higher LHE could be achieved. According to Fig. 4, the EG of F3Os was higher than 5 eV, which gave an insulation property to it. The EG reduced more than 60% after combining F3Os with GQDs. A semiconducting property was induced due to the significant decrease in the EG, which improved LHE and electron excitation. After doping N, there was a decrease in the EG of G-F3Os (2.93 eV) to 2.87–1.60 eV. The decrease in the F3Os EG of N1-G-F3Os was the highest, which was 54%. In general, doping N resulted in lower EG, which showed that the efficient tuning of the EG of the NCs was possible. There was an inverse relationship between the absorption wave lengths and the EGs. As a result, we can change the absorption to a shorter or longer wavelength by tuning the EG. Accordingly, we expected the NCs to exhibit a red-shift in the absorption wavelengths to the infrared (IR) region.

As a trivalent atom, N is capable of forming 3 bonds with C and leaving1 vacancy on the 4th site of C. Doping the N atom can create holes in the lattice of GQDs which serve as a charge carrier. This is commonly referred to as p-type doping, where, within the EG above the HOMOs;, new un-occupied energy states are created, which reduces the EG [55]. These newly created energy states act as electron acceptors from the HOMO level, thus leaving vacancies. The numbers of vacancies created are equal to those of energy states created. The DOS of edge and center doping with N is shown in Fig. 4. As can be seen, a new unoccupied energy state was created by N doping above the HOMO. There was an overlap between the HOMO and the newly created energy state in the edge (N2-G-F3Os), but the newly created energy state in the center doping (N1-G-F3Os) was higher than the HOMO, which caused the EG of center doping (1.60 eV) to be narrower compared to that of the edge doping (2.87 eV). Accordingly, the red-shift of the central doping could be larger in the UV–visible absorption spectrum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–visible absorption as well as emission transition analysis
ce:section.ce:para: One of the significant factors which enhances the efficiency of DYSCs is their optical attributes. Absorption has an impact on the current generation which has a direct relationship with efficiency. A photosensitizer should possess a strong absorption in the range of 400 to 800 nm (the VR) because it is in the range of solar radiations. So, it can absorb photons that excite their electrons. Initiating current generation requires electrons to be excited, which are injected into the C-B of the photoanodes and begin flowing as a current. The absorption maximum wavelength of all NCs at CPCM-M06-2X/6–31 + G(d,p) in methanol can be found in Table 1 . In the U-V region (100–300 nm), F3Os exhibited a maximum absorption at 215 nm, which was similar to the experimental results (280 nm) with only a 65 nm difference. Hybridizing catechin with GQDs caused a shift in the absorption to longer wavelengths from the U-V region to the VR at 460 nm, which showed the possibility of using G-F3Os as a photosensitizer. As shown in Table 1, the shift could be attributed to decrease in the EG of F3Os. Central N -doped G-F3Os (N1-G-F3Os) caused a red-shift from 460 nm to 483 nm, whereas edge doping with N (N2-G-F3Os) led to a negligible redshift to 462 nm. As expected from the FMOs and DOS plots, N1-G-F3Os exhibited longer wavelengths compared to N2-G-F3Os. Nevertheless, N2-G-F3Os and N1-G-F3Os exhibited absorption in the VR contrary to F3Os, which demonstrates the possibility utilizing them as a photosensitizer.

There is a direct relationship between square of the transition dipole moment, which causes the occurrence of transition, and oscillator strength [56]. The probability of transition gets higher when the oscillator strength becomes higher. Also, it associated with LHE that rises when there is an increase in the oscillator strength. When LHE is enhanced, photon-capturing ability also enhances which can produce more current. As shown in Table 1, G-F3Os exhibited transition at 460 nm and the oscillator strength was 1.37. The intensity of this transition was more than that of F3Os. This improvement caused the LHE to increase from 0.48 to 0.96. So, G-F3Os has a higher photon-capturing ability. There was a decrease in the absorption intensity 0.86 and 0.79, respectively, for N1-G-F3Os and N2-G-F3Os, which caused a reduction in the LHE as well. The absorption intensity and LHE of G-F3Os was the highest. Nonetheless, the LHE of N1-G-F3Os and N2-G-F3Os was near unity, which increased their photon-capturing ability. Table 2 provides information on short circuit current density, excited state lifetime, oscillator strength, excitation energy and the maximum emission wavelengths. Stokes shift can be defined as the difference between emission peaks and the absorption. When Stokes shift is larger, it means that the ICT character is stronger and the difference between the geometries of ground state and excited state is larger [57]. The Stokes shift was in the range of59 nm (G-F3Os) to 74 nm (N1-G-F3Os). We also observed that there was a further increase in the Stokes shift after doping the NCs, which shows that doping contributed to the shift in the emission peaks into longer wavelengths.

According to Eq. (5), there is a direct relationship between JSC and PCE, which means that more current generation is capable of enhancing the PCE of DYSCs. As shown in Table 2, the JSC of both NCs ranged from 9.38 mA.cm −2 to 16.56 mA.cm - 2. As could be seen, the JSC of N1-G-F3Os was 16.56 mAcm−2, which was the highest. This could be because of its higher photon flux. Unlike N2-G-F3Os, the capability of edge and central N doping in current generation was less (11.18 mA.cm −2). Thus, the PCE achieved by central doping (N1-G-F3Os) could be higher than the PCE achieved by edge doping (N2-G-F3Os). Additionally, the current generation of N1-G-F3Os was the highest, which shows that it also has the highest PCE. This shows that it is the most ideal and suitable nano-composite.

The current work investigated the possibility of using F3Os and its NCs as a photosensitizer in DYSCs by theoretically performing DFT and TD-DFT calculations. Also, it investigated the impact of doing GQDs with the nitrogen atom and its hybridization. The positive BE values demonstrated the stability of all the NCs. The molecular orbitals, HOMOs and LUMOs, respectively, of NCs had a suitable alignment with the reduction potential and conduction band. The HOMO of N2-G-F3Os was the nearest to the reduction potential and LUMO of N1-G-F3Os was the nearest to the CB, which rendered them the most suitable electron-transferring NCs. There was a noticeable decrease in the EG of F3Os for matching the wavelengths in the VR, which changed the F3Os (215 eV) absorption from the U-V region to the VR in the range of 483 eV. So, the LHE of most doped NCs was higher compared to the LHE of un-doped NCs. This raised the current generation. Moreover, the doped systems generated more current compared to undoped systems. N1-G-F3Os generated the highest JSC of 16.56 mA.cm −2. A higher current generation can improve the PCE of DYSCs.

Anjan Kumar: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software. Sabah Auda Abdul Ameer: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software. Sura Mohammad Mohealdeen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software. Ahmed Hasoon: Methodology, Software, Writing – review & editing. Yousra Ali Abdulsayed: Methodology, Software, Writing – review & editing. Abdelmajeed Adam Lagum: Methodology, Software, Writing – review & editing. Alaa M. Al-Ma'abreh: Methodology, Software, Writing – review & editing. Mustafa M. Kadhim: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficiency enhancement of dye-sensitized solar cells based on Gracilaria/Ulva using graphene quantum dot

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Saedi, A.M. Moradi, S. Kimiagar, H.A. Panahi, Efficiency enhancement of dye-sensitized solar cells based on Gracilaria/Ulva using graphene quantum dot, International Journal of Environmental Research, 14 (2020) 393-402.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effects of the coupling between electrode and GQD-anthoxanthin nanocomposites for dye-sensitized solar cell: DFT and TD-DFT investigations

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Gao, C.-L. Yang, G. Jiang, Effects of the coupling between electrode and GQD-anthoxanthin nanocomposites for dye-sensitized solar cell: DFT and TD-DFT investigations, Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry, 407 (2021) 113080.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Natural dyes and their effect on efficiency of TiO2 based DSSCs: a comparative study

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.K. Singh, B. Koiry, Natural dyes and their effect on efficiency of TiO2 based DSSCs: a comparative study, Materials Today: Proceedings, 5 (2018) 2112-2122.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Performance improvement of dye-sensitized solar cell-(DSSC-) based natural dyes by clathrin protein
